summary: Iranian-born photographer, video artist, and filmmaker. Best known for her Women of Allah photo series (1993-1997), which features veiled women with guns and Farsi calligraphy inscribed on their skin. Also known for a trilogy of video installations: Turbulence, Rapture, and Fervor. Her work explores themes of gender, identity, and the effects of the 1979 Iranian Revolution.
comprehensive_summary: Shirin Neshat is an Iranian-born visual artist who works primarily in photography and video. She left Iran before the 1979 Revolution and returned years later to find a transformed country, which deeply influenced her art. Her most frequently referenced work in quiz bowl is the Women of Allah series, a collection of photographs depicting veiled Iranian women holding guns, with their exposed skin inscribed with Farsi calligraphy (often Quranic verses). This series is noted for eschewing conventional calligraphic styles like naskh. She also produced a trilogy of video installations titled Turbulence, Rapture, and Fervor. Her work is frequently clued alongside other Iranian artists and in the context of Islamic calligraphy and the Hurufiyya movement.

works.name: Women of Allah (1993-1997)
works.description: A series of photographs depicting veiled Iranian women with guns, their exposed skin inscribed with Farsi calligraphy. The series explored women's lives before and after the 1979 Iranian Revolution. Succeeded Neshat's earlier work Unveiling. Wikipedia
